youtube_items.title: [YT] Asteroid Mining Is No Longer Sci-Fi: TransAstra, AstroForge & Karman+ Explained
youtube_items.summary: Today I'm looking at the asteroid startup scene. I'll cover a few of the startups with plans to mine asteroids for metals or volatiles and return them to Earth orbit for sale. Several missions have demonstrated collecting and returning asteroidal material for study on Earth. Both Hayabusa 1 and 2 plus NASA's Osiris Rex were able to collect materials from the surface of an asteroid deep space. However, these missions were very expensive. Osiris Rex, for example, cost over $1.3 billion just to return 121 grams of material. Asteroids can provide fuel, water, and precious metals for use in space, perhaps as feed stockck in orbital manufacturing, or to fuel space tugs, commercial space stations, or satellites. I'll finish with my thoughts on the current state of the asteroid mining sector. Afte

raw_items.title: Asteroid mining
raw_items.summary: Asteroid mining is the hypothetical and technically possible extraction of materials from asteroids and other minor planets, including near-Earth objects.Research missions focused on asteroid sample return, including Hayabusa, Hayabusa2, OSIRIS-REx, and Tianwen-2, illustrate the challenges of collecting ore from space using current technology. As of 2024, around 127 grams of asteroid material have

raw_items.title: What Is Asteroid Mining and Is It Actually Possible?
raw_items.summary: Mar 20, 2026 · Asteroid mining is the concept of extracting useful materials, such as water, metals, and rare earth elements, from asteroids in space. It remains largely theoretical, but the industry is moving from PowerPoint slides to actual spacecraft.

raw_items.title: Space Resource Exploration and Utilization Act of 2015
raw_items.summary: The Space Resource Exploration and Utilization Act of 2015 (H.R. 1508) was a bill (which never became law) introduced in the 114th Congress of the U.S. on March 19, 2015, by Rep. Bill Posey, Republican of Florida. It is identical to the Senate bill by the same name (S. 976).
